title: Weather update: Rajasthan, Gujarat to see heatwave; storms, hail and snowfall likely in Himalayan region - CNBC TV18

full_text:
3 Min Read
India’s weather pattern this week is splitting into two very different tracks. Parts of western and northwestern India are moving into heatwave territory, while the northern mountains and the Northeast are preparing for rain, thunderstorms and even hail.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) says both systems are already active. Temperatures are climbing quickly across several western states, while a Western Disturbance is bringing unstable weather to the Himalayan belt.
Heatwave conditions in western India
IMD has warned of heatwave conditions over Rajasthan, Gujarat and Vidarbha on March 10 and March 11. North Konkan is also likely to experience heatwave conditions today.
Temperatures in some areas have already crossed the 40°C mark. The highest maximum temperature recorded on Monday was 41.6°C in Rajkot and Surendranagar in Gujarat.
Across several northern regions, including Delhi, Punjab, Himachal Pradesh and Jammu & Kashmir, daytime temperatures are around 5°C above normal.
The Met Office expects these elevated temperatures to continue for another couple of days before easing slightly. A gradual drop of around 2-3°C is likely over the northwest plains beginning around the third day of the forecast period.
Rain, snow and hail expected in the Himalayan region
A Western Disturbance is influencing weather over the western Himalayan region.
Jammu & Kashmir is expected to receive heavy rainfall and snowfall on March 10 and 11, along with thunderstorms and possible hail.
In Himachal Pradesh, thunderstorms and hailstorms are likely between March 11 and March 12. Gusty winds reaching 40-50 kmph may accompany these storms.
Weather activity in the hills may increase through March 12, weaken briefly, and then strengthen again over parts of Himachal Pradesh between March 14 and March 16.
Thunderstorms forecast for Northeast
Thunderstorms with lightning are expected across Arunachal Pradesh, Assam and Meghalaya, and Nagaland-Manipur-Mizoram-Tripura through March 15.
Rainfall in Sub-Himalayan West Bengal and Sikkim is also expected during this period, with scattered to fairly widespread showers.
Towards the west, Bihar and Jharkhand may see thunderstorms and gusty winds of 30–40 kmph on March 10 and 11.
Mostly dry conditions across central and southern India
Most of central and peninsular India - this includes Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Karnataka and Odisha - is expected to remain largely dry over the next four to five days.
Kerala and Tamil Nadu may experience isolated showers - but widespread rainfall is not expected.
Overall, the IMD says India will continue to see a mix of rising heat in the west and unsettled weather in the hills and Northeast through the middle of the week.
